ocr.text: In the ongoing investigation of imaging modalities for clinical applications, the comparative analysis between patients and
control groups remains critical in understanding diagnostic efficacy. The data presented in the accompanying table elucidates
several key metrics that inform our evaluation of both deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity and specificity. Specifically, the
columns labeled “Deterministic Sensitivity” and “Probabilistic Sensitivity” provide insight into the accuracy of imaging

techniques under various conditions. 

The data indicates that the deterministic sensitivity is notably higher than its probabilistic
counterpart, suggesting that traditional methodologies may yield more reliable results in specific contexts. This distinction is
particularly relevant when considering the field of view (FOV) utilized during imaging, as a broader FOV can significantly
impact sensitivity metrics.
HaplotypeMyD88 -938C>A SNP1-SNP2  Estimated frequencies  p value  p value global
Patients  Controls
Observer  FOV  mA  DeterministicSensitivity  Probabilistic Sensitivity  Deterministic Specificity  ProbabilisticSpecificity
Furthermore, the image acquisition parameters, such as milliampere-seconds (mA), directly influence the quality of the

resulting images, thereby affecting diagnostic accuracy.

The importance of these metrics cannot be overstated, as they directly
correlate with the potential outcomes for patient management and treatment planning. By analyzing the differences between
patients and controls, we can better comprehend the implications of these imaging techniques on clinical decision-making
processes. The findings underscore the necessity for further research to refine these metrics and enhance the reliability of
diagnostic imaging in patient care, ultimately leading to improved health outcomes.
